one_line_takeaway: By reframing a harmful request as a principle-isomorphic benign one, eliciting a detailed benign response, and then inverting it back to harmful content via the target model itself, Safe2Harm achieves higher black-box jailbreak ASR (e.g., 79.8-97.98% across several models/datasets) than five prior attacks while converging in 1-2 queries instead of tens to thousands of iterations.
key_finding: On Llama-3-8B-Instruct (Table 2), Safe2Harm achieved ASR of 79.8% (JailbreakBench), 87.0% (AdvBench), and 81.3% (HarmBench), beating all five baselines (best baseline AutoDAN-Turbo reached only 35.0/31.4/37.2% respectively). On the JailbreakBench per-model breakdown (Table 4), Safe2Harm reached 97.98% ASR on both Gemini-2.5-Flash and DeepSeek, and 67.00% on GPT-5 (vs. best baseline PAIR at 24.15% on GPT-5). Unlike other attacks, whose ASR fell as Qwen3 model scale increased from 1.7B to 8B, Safe2Harm's ASR rose with scale, attributed to larger models executing the rewriting/mapping steps more accurately. Qwen3 'Thinking Mode' increased ASR for most attacks, and the paper links this to longer chain-of-thought length correlating with harmful outputs (Fig. 4), suggesting Thinking Mode weakens safety focus in favor of task completion. On iteration efficiency, Safe2Harm converges in 1-2 attempts versus PAIR's ~2-3 iterations x 30 parallel streams, JAIL-CON's ~10 iterations, AutoDAN/AutoDAN-Turbo's 60-125 iterations, and GCG's hundreds-to-thousands of iterations. On the harmful-content-detection benchmark (Table 3), Qwen3Guard 4B was the best detector overall: 84.08% accuracy, 5.31% FPR, 9.50% FNR; Qwen3Guard 8B scored 81.84% accuracy/7.82% FPR/9.22% FNR; GPT-5 and DeepSeek scored ~70% accuracy; keyword-based detection scored 60.89% accuracy; the Llama-Prompt-Guard-2 models performed worst (49.16%/41.34% accuracy).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is Safe2Harm, a black-box jailbreak attack against LLM safety alignment that exploits what it calls 'semantic isomorphism' between harmful and legitimate scenarios (e.g., an incendiary bomb and a fire-training combustion simulator share the same underlying fuel-oxidizer-ignition principle). The attack runs in four stages, each implemented via LLM calls: (1) Harmful Question Rewriting — an auxiliary LLM (Qwen-Plus) rewrites the harmful question into a semantically 'safe' question sharing the same underlying principle, so it does not trigger refusal; (2) Topic Extraction and Mapping — the auxiliary LLM extracts a bidirectional topic-pair mapping M = (t_harm, t_safe) between the original and rewritten questions; (3) Safe Response Generation — the safe question is sent to the actual target LLM, which produces a detailed response since it appears benign; (4) Theme Inversion and Rewriting — the target LLM itself (not the auxiliary model) is prompted to invert the safe response back into harmful terms using mapping M, yielding the final harmful output. A Doubao-based LLM-judge quality-control loop (max 3 retries per stage) checks each stage's output for principle consistency before proceeding. The paper also independently contributes a 358-sample 'challenging harmful content evaluation dataset' (195 safe / 163 harmful) built by pooling attack responses from Safe2Harm and five baselines, majority-voting across DeepSeek/GPT-5/Doubao/Qwen3Guard, then manually filtering for ambiguous cases (harm hidden in role-play, boundary-straddling content, mixed harmful/safe text), used to benchmark existing harmful-content detectors.
threat_model: Black-box attacker with only API/query access to a target LLM (no gradients, no white-box weights needed for the core attack, though the paper also tests white-box GCG/AutoDAN as transfer baselines). The attacker uses auxiliary LLM calls (Qwen-Plus for stages 1-3, the target model itself for stage 4) to reformulate a harmful request as a semantically parallel benign one, extract a harmful-to-safe topic mapping, elicit a detailed benign-looking response from the target model, and then invert that response back to harmful content — bypassing the target's input-level safety classifier by never presenting it with an overtly harmful prompt, and bypassing output-level review because the inversion happens outside the target model's own generation-time safety check (stage 4 uses the target LLM again, but the paper's framing is that by that point the model is just performing an instructed text transformation rather than judging harmfulness of a request).
limitations: Stated by the paper itself: (1) Safe2Harm has not been tested on Vision-Language Models, only text LLMs — left as future work; (2) for 'extremely harmful' requests (the paper's example: generating explicit pornographic content), the attack often fails because the theme-inversion stage is itself rejected by the target model, and the authors propose future fine-tuning-assisted mapping optimization to address this. Not explicitly stated but evident from the method: the approach depends on being able to find a plausible 'legitimate' isomorphic scenario for the harmful request, which may not exist for some harm categories; white-box baseline comparisons on large models used transferred (not directly optimized) adversarial suffixes, which the paper flags as a fairness caveat rather than hides.

datasets_used: Self-constructed 358-sample harmful content evaluation dataset (195 safe / 163 harmful)
quantitative_results: Safe2Harm ASR on GPT-5 / Gemini-2.5-Flash / DeepSeek on JailbreakBench: 67.00% / 97.98% / 97.98% (Table 4)
Safe2Harm converges in 1-2 iterations vs PAIR (2-3 iters x 30 streams), JAIL-CON (~10 iters), AutoDAN/AutoDAN-Turbo (60-125 iters), GCG (hundreds to thousands of iters)
Harmful-content detection: Qwen3Guard 4B best overall at 84.08% accuracy / 5.31% FPR / 9.50% FNR (Table 3)
Harmful-content detection: GPT-5 70.39% accuracy / 7.54% FPR / 20.39% FNR; DeepSeek 70.67% / 8.66% / 19.55% (Table 3)
Harmful-content detection: Keyword-based detection 60.89% accuracy / 14.25% FPR / 23.74% FNR (Table 3)
Harmful-content detection: Llama-Prompt-Guard-2-22M 49.16% accuracy / 12.29% FPR / 37.43% FNR; -86M variant 41.34% / 34.07% / 23.46% (Table 3)
